name: Darjeeling Himalayan Foothills

staple_foods.name: Darjeeling first flush tea

primary_metabolites.protein_content: ~24 g/100g dry leaf [tea leaf protein — FDC proxy for dried tea]
primary_metabolites.ascorbic_acid: 250-300 mg/100g dry leaf [green tea — Deka & Bhatt 2013 J Food Sci Tech 50:874]

key_flavor_bioactives.caffeine_concentration: 2.5-3.8 g/100g dry leaf [Darjeeling first flush — Deka & Bhatt 2013 J Food Sci Tech 50:874]
key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 18.0-22.0 g/100g dry leaf as catechins [Darjeeling high-altitude — Kim et al. 2011 Food Chem 129:486]
key_flavor_bioactives.tannin_content: 8.0-12.0 g/100g dry leaf [Darjeeling EGCG+ECG — Kim et al. 2011]

terpenes.linalool: 15-35 mg/kg dry leaf [Darjeeling muscatel aroma — mono- and diterpene alcohols; Ravichandran & Parthiban 1998 Food Chem 62:77]

organic_acids.malic_acid: 0.5-1.2 g/100g dry leaf [tea plant organic acids — altitude-responsive; Hilal & Engelhardt 2007 J Consum Prot Food Saf 2:414]
organic_acids.citric_acid: 0.2-0.6 g/100g dry leaf [Camellia sinensis — Hilal & Engelhardt 2007]

umami_compounds.glutamate: 1.0-2.0 g/100g dry leaf as theanine+glutamate [L-theanine 1.5-2.5 g/100g at high altitude — Park et al. 2010 Amino Acids 39:843; shading and altitude elevate theanine]

staple_foods.notes: Darjeeling first flush tea is the high-altitude anchor for the 3-RSU tea natural experiment (RSU-20 at 2000m, RSU-21 Assam at 100m, RSU-22 Uji Japan at 300m). The 'muscatel' character of Darjeeling is driven by linalool oxide and geraniol produced by leafhopper (Jacobiasca formosana) herbivory stress — an insect-mediated terroir signal unique to high-altitude, cool conditions. Catechin (EGCG) content is strongly altitude-dependent: 18-22 g/100g at 2000m vs 12-15 g/100g in Assam lowlands. L-theanine content is also elevated at altitude (cool temperature reduces theanine breakdown). NOTE: 'glutamate' field here represents the theanine+glutamate pool — L-theanine is the dominant form and is a distinct metabolite from free glutamate.
data_sources: Deka & Bhatt (2013) — Caffeine and catechins in Darjeeling tea, J Food Sci Tech 50:874
Kim et al. (2011) — Catechin profiles in Darjeeling, Food Chem 129:486
Ravichandran & Parthiban (1998) — Volatile aroma compounds in Darjeeling, Food Chem 62:77
Hilal & Engelhardt (2007) — Organic acids in Camellia sinensis, J Consum Prot Food Saf 2:414
Park et al. (2010) — L-theanine in tea by altitude, Amino Acids 39:843

staple_foods.name: buckwheat

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 400-700 mg rutin/100g [buckwheat — rutin is the dominant flavonoid; Kreft et al. 2006 Food Chem 98:508]

staple_foods.notes: Buckwheat is the highland carbohydrate of Darjeeling and Sikkim — grown above 1500m where wheat and rice cultivation is marginal. Rutin (quercetin-3-rutinoside) is the key bioactive — a flavonoid with documented altitude-elevation response due to UV stress. Comparable to RSU-01 (Boreal Finland) oats as a non-wheat highland grain.
data_sources: Kreft et al. (2006) — Rutin in buckwheat, Food Chem 98:508

staple_foods.name: churpi yak cheese

primary_metabolites.protein_content: 30-45 g/100g [hard dried churpi — Tamang & Sarkar 1996 J General Appl Microbiol 42:101]

umami_compounds.glutamate: 1.0-2.5 g/100g [aged churpi — protein hydrolysis during prolonged drying; Tamang & Sarkar 1996]

staple_foods.notes: Churpi is the traditional hard yak cheese of the Eastern Himalaya — extraordinarily dense (stone-hard when dried), sucked over hours as a protein ration by herders. Produced by pressing defatted yak milk curd and sun-drying at altitude. The prolonged protein hydrolysis produces elevated free glutamate — a form of natural MSG extraction. Very high altitude protein source.
data_sources: Tamang & Sarkar (1996) — Microflora of traditional fermented foods of Himalaya, J General Appl Microbiol 42:101

staple_foods.name: gundruk fermented greens

primary_metabolites.protein_content: 2.5-3.5 g/100g [gundruk — Tamang et al. 2005 Int J Food Microbiol 101:131]

key_flavor_bioactives.isothiocyanates: present — Brassica leaf substrate contains glucosinolate precursors [Tamang et al. 2005]

organic_acids.lactic_acid: 1.5-2.5 g/100g [gundruk LAB — Tamang et al. 2005 Int J Food Microbiol 101:131]

staple_foods.notes: Gundruk is the traditional fermented leafy vegetable of Nepal and Darjeeling — wilted mustard or radish leaves fermented in a pit or sealed container for 2-3 weeks, then sun-dried. LAB fermentation produces high lactic acid. Glucosinolate compounds (isothiocyanates) from the Brassica substrate are partially preserved. This is the only RSU in the pilot set with confirmed isothiocyanates as a bioactive — a potential distinct signal for cruciferous-vegetable-fermentation cultures.
data_sources: Tamang et al. (2005) — Gundruk microbiology, Int J Food Microbiol 101:131

staple_foods.name: ghee
staple_foods.notes: Ghee (clarified butter) is the traditional cooking fat across South Asian highland cultures. Himalayan yak or cow ghee may differ in fatty acid composition from lowland cow ghee — yak ghee is higher in CLA and omega-3. FDC 173577 is regular cow ghee and used as proxy.

notes: Darjeeling / Eastern Himalayan foothills (West Bengal, India, ~2000m). Metamorphic parent material (gneiss, schist) with highly acidic, fast-draining soils. This is the high-altitude tea anchor for the natural experiment with RSU-21 (Assam, 100m) and RSU-22 (Uji/Shizuoka Japan, 300m). The altitude-driven catechin elevation (18-22 vs 12-15 g/100g), linalool muscatel aroma, and theanine elevation are well-documented in the literature and are among the most publishable altitude-metabolite signals in the pilot set. Gundruk fermented greens are a unique signal: Brassica-based, high-lactic-acid fermentation culture not represented elsewhere.
